site.name: 使用说明书
site.description: wunder is an agent orchestration system for organizations and individual users. / wunder 是一个面向组织和个人用户的智能体调度系统。

pages.title: wunder 心舰
pages.summary: wunder 是统一承载 server、cli、desktop 三种运行形态的智能体调度系统；入口建议先按角色和目标分流，再进入具体文档。

pages.title: 快速开始
pages.summary: 用最短路径跑通 wunder 的第一条可用链路；默认推荐 desktop，其次按 server 或 cli 分流。

pages.title: Desktop 入门
pages.summary: 先把 Wunder 用起来时，优先看 desktop；它是个人用户和本地优先场景的默认入口。

pages.title: Server 部署
pages.summary: 需要多用户治理、统一接入层和管理员后台时，再看 `wunder-server` 这条线。

pages.title: CLI 使用
pages.summary: 需要终端执行、脚本化接入或开发调试时，再看 `wunder-cli`。

pages.title: 说明书总览
pages.summary: 按角色和任务快速定位使用说明书入口，避免在全站里盲搜。

pages.title: 常用说明目录
pages.summary: 这是 wunder 使用说明书里最常用页面的精选目录。

pages.title: 核心概览
pages.summary: 11 个核心不是 11 个功能点，而是 Wunder 为了长期运行、多人并发和多入口接入而主动建立的系统骨架。

pages.title: 智能体循环
pages.summary: 智能体循环解释了 Wunder 为什么要把线程当作一等运行单元，以及为什么执行主链路必须稳定、可恢复、可收敛。

pages.title: 工具
pages.summary: 工具核心解释了 Wunder 为什么要把能力统一收口成工具，以及为什么工具必须同时面向模型调用、执行治理和前端展示。

pages.title: 蜂群
pages.summary: 蜂群核心解释 Wunder 如何把多智能体协作建成正式系统能力，而不是把并行任务临时塞进一个大线程里。

pages.title: 上下文压缩
pages.summary: 上下文压缩核心解释 Wunder 如何在长会话里主动治理上下文规模，并把“压缩”设计成可追溯、可观测、可复盘的正式行为。

pages.title: 记忆
pages.summary: 记忆核心解释 Wunder 为什么坚持“线程初始化注入一次、后续保持冻结”，并把长期资料利用与线程认知稳定性分开治理。

pages.title: 渠道
pages.summary: 渠道核心解释 Wunder 为什么允许多入口接入，但绝不允许这些入口长成多套独立运行时。

pages.title: 定时任务
pages.summary: 定时任务核心解释 Wunder 为什么要把时间驱动的后台任务建成正式能力，而不是把它们丢给外部脚本临时处理。

pages.title: 多用户管理
pages.summary: 多用户管理核心解释 Wunder 为什么必须从一开始就把组织、用户、权限和 Token 账户治理放进系统骨架，而不是后补。

pages.title: 实时性
pages.summary: 实时性核心解释 Wunder 为什么必须把线程变化持续投影给外部，并严格区分事件投影与后端真相。

pages.title: 稳定性
pages.summary: 稳定性核心解释 Wunder 为什么必须用结构性治理来控制故障扩散，而不是把稳定性寄托在 prompt、好运气和人工盯盘上。

pages.title: 可观测性
pages.summary: 可观测性核心解释 Wunder 如何把“发生了什么、为什么、如何复盘”拆成事实层、回放层和画像层三套明确语义。

pages.title: 接入概览
pages.summary: Wunder 的接入面包含统一执行入口、聊天域、工作区、渠道、A2A 与 MCP；先分层选型，接入会更稳。

pages.title: wunder API
pages.summary: `/wunder` 是统一执行入口，适合能力调用；当你要做完整聊天产品时，优先切到 `/wunder/chat/*`。

pages.title: 聊天 WebSocket
pages.summary: `/wunder/chat/ws` 是 Wunder 聊天主实时通道；实时会话优先 WS，SSE 作为兜底。

pages.title: 聊天会话
pages.summary: `/wunder/chat/sessions/*` 是 Wunder 聊天主域，负责会话生命周期、消息收发、附件预处理、事件恢复和运行态管理。

pages.title: 工作区 API
pages.summary: `/wunder/workspace*` 不只是文件接口，它决定文件到底落在哪个隔离工作区。

pages.title: 临时目录与文档转换
pages.summary: `/wunder/temp_dir/*` 负责临时上传、下载和中转；文档转换与聊天附件预处理分别走公共转换、调试转换和聊天域转换接口。

pages.title: 用户世界接口
pages.summary: `/wunder/user_world/*` 是 Wunder 里的用户到用户通信域，与用户到智能体会话域并行存在。

pages.title: 渠道 Webhook
pages.summary: Wunder 用 `/wunder/channel/{provider}/webhook` 统一承接外部渠道入站，再交给 ChannelHub 和 outbox 处理。

pages.title: 微信 iLink 渠道
pages.summary: `weixin` 是 Wunder 的新微信渠道（iLink 协议），与 `wechat` / `wechat_mp` 不同；本文给出选型、扫码接入、运行态检查与故障定位。

pages.title: 外部登录与免登嵌入
pages.summary: Wunder 保留 `/wunder/auth/external/*` 作为外部系统嵌入和免登接入面。

pages.title: A2A 接口
pages.summary: Wunder 通过 `/a2a` 提供 A2A JSON-RPC 标准接入，并通过 AgentCard 暴露能力发现入口。

pages.title: MCP 入口
pages.summary: Wunder 同时支持自托管 MCP 服务 `/wunder/mcp` 与外部 MCP 服务接入。

pages.title: 界面概览
pages.summary: Wunder 当前同时维护用户侧前端、管理端前端和桌面端三块界面表面，它们共享同一套后端能力，但职责不同。

pages.title: 用户侧前端
pages.summary: 用户侧前端位于 `frontend/`，已经收敛为统一 Messenger 工作台，并覆盖资料、聊天、蜂群、工作区和系统设置。

pages.title: 管理端界面
pages.summary: 管理端前端位于 `web/`，承担模型、工具、用户、渠道与运行治理。

pages.title: Desktop 界面
pages.summary: `wunder-desktop` 是当前主交付形态，强调本地优先与桌面工作台。

pages.title: 运维概览
pages.summary: Wunder 运维包含部署、存储、安全、渠道运行态与可观测；目标是让系统可持续稳定运行，而不只是「启动成功」。

pages.title: 部署与运行
pages.summary: 部署 Wunder 前先分清 desktop、本地开发和 server 三条路径，再谈启动命令。

pages.title: 数据与存储
pages.summary: Wunder 的持久化需要同时分清数据库、工作区、向量存储和临时目录。

pages.title: Desktop 本地模式
pages.summary: Desktop 本地模式不是缩小版 server，而是一套本地优先、持久目录优先的运行形态。

pages.title: 认证与安全
pages.summary: Wunder 需要同时区分 API Key、用户 Token、外链鉴权和工具执行边界。

pages.title: 监控与 Benchmark
pages.summary: Wunder 的观测面不是单一监控页，而是由会话监控、工具统计、性能采样、吞吐压测和 benchmark 共同组成。

pages.title: 渠道运行态
pages.summary: 渠道问题很多时候不在模型，而在账号、长连接、Webhook、outbox 和下载链接改写这条接入链路里。

pages.title: 参考概览
pages.summary: 参考页提供稳定查表内容，也收纳按主题拆开的运行模型参考页，适合在核心总览之后深入细节。

pages.title: API 索引
pages.summary: 这是 Wunder 当前最常用接口族的索引页，用来快速定位入口而不是替代完整 API 文档。

pages.title: 配置说明
pages.summary: 配置不生效时，先确认你改的是哪一层，而不是先怀疑程序没读到。

pages.title: 流式事件参考
pages.summary: 接 Wunder 的流式链路时，最重要的不是把每个事件都背下来，而是知道哪些事件承担真正的生命周期语义。

pages.title: 工作区路由参考
pages.summary: Wunder 的工作区路由不是单纯按 user_id 取目录，而是由 `container_id`、智能体容器配置和 scoped user_id 共同决定。

pages.title: 提示词模板参考
pages.summary: Wunder 将系统提示词拆成模板包和分段文件。管理员负责系统模板包，用户侧现在默认提供中英两套内置模板包，并支持按需切换或复制为自定义包。

pages.title: 管理端面板索引
pages.summary: 管理员侧前端不是单一调试页，而是一组分工明确的治理面板。

pages.title: 系统架构
pages.summary: wunder 通过统一调度内核承载 server、cli、desktop 三种运行形态。

pages.title: 工作区与容器
pages.summary: Wunder 的文件隔离不是“一个当前目录”，而是按 `user_id + container_id` 分层组织的持久化工作区。

pages.title: 会话与轮次
pages.summary: wunder 将对话拆分为会话、线程、用户轮次与模型轮次。

pages.title: 流式执行
pages.summary: Wunder 的流式不是简单的字符流，而是把线程执行、工具调用和终态一起暴露出来。

pages.title: 运行时与在线状态
pages.summary: Wunder 不只返回消息内容，还持续维护线程和会话的运行时投影，用于表达 loaded、waiting、streaming、idle 等状态。

pages.title: 工具体系
pages.summary: Wunder 的核心不是只让模型回答，而是让模型在同一调度层里选择并编排工具。

pages.title: 提示词与技能
pages.summary: Wunder 把系统提示词和技能都当成正式治理对象，而不是散落在代码里的临时文本。

pages.title: 长期记忆
pages.summary: wunder 的长期记忆围绕线程冻结、单次注入与结构化记忆碎片设计。

pages.title: Token 账户与占用
pages.summary: Wunder 用 Token 账户管理用户可支配余额，用 Token 占用观察上下文负载；这两者相关，但不是一回事。

pages.title: 蜂群协作
pages.summary: wunder 的蜂群能力用于多智能体协作，核心是母蜂编排、工蜂执行、必要时再派生子智能体。

pages.title: 边界处理
pages.summary: wunder 在上下文超限、网络中断、工具失败、系统异常等边界场景下的处理策略与恢复机制。

pages.title: 帮助
pages.summary: 先按症状分流，再进入排障路径；目标是减少盲查日志和重复试错。

pages.title: 故障排查
pages.summary: 按“入口 -> 鉴权 -> 配置 -> 依赖 -> 实时通道”顺序排查，可快速定位大部分 Wunder 故障。

pages.title: FAQ
pages.summary: wunder 的高频问题快速答复，适合在进入详细排障前先做判断。

pages.summary: 这页用于快速统一 Wunder 文档里的核心术语，避免把 user、session、thread、container、agent 混成一个概念。

pages.title: wunder
pages.summary: wunder is an agent orchestration system that unifies three runtime forms: server, cli, and desktop. Start by choosing the path that matches your role and goal.

pages.title: Quickstart
pages.summary: Run your first usable wunder flow with the shortest path. Desktop is the default recommendation, followed by server or cli depending on your role.

pages.title: Desktop Guide
pages.summary: If your first goal is simply to start using wunder, begin with desktop. It is the default entry point for individual and local-first use.

pages.title: Server Deployment
pages.summary: Start with `wunder-server` only when you need multi-user governance, a unified access layer, and an admin backend.

pages.title: CLI Usage
pages.summary: Read this page when you need terminal execution, scripted integration, or developer-oriented debugging through `wunder-cli`.

pages.title: Manual Hubs
pages.summary: Jump to the right part of the manual by role and task instead of searching the whole site blindly.

pages.title: Frequently Used Pages
pages.summary: A curated directory of the most frequently used pages in the wunder manual.

pages.title: Core Concepts
pages.summary: The eleven core concepts are not eleven feature bullets. They are the structural boundaries wunder establishes for long-running threads, concurrent users, and multiple entry surfaces.

pages.title: Core Agent Loop
pages.summary: The Core Agent Loop explains why Wunder treats threads as first-class runtime units, and why the main execution path must be stable, recoverable, and convergent.

pages.title: Tools
pages.summary: The Tools core explains why Wunder must unify all capabilities as tools, and why tools must simultaneously serve model invocation, execution governance, and frontend display.

pages.title: Swarm
pages.summary: The Swarm core explains how Wunder builds multi-agent collaboration into a formal system capability, rather than temporarily stuffing parallel tasks into a single large thread.

pages.title: Context Compression
pages.summary: The Context Compression core explains how Wunder actively manages context size during long sessions, making compression a traceable, observable, and reviewable formal operation.

pages.title: Memory
pages.summary: The Memory core explains why Wunder insists on "inject once during thread initialization, keep frozen afterward", separating long-term data utilization from thread cognitive stability.

pages.title: Core Channels
pages.summary: Core Channels explains why Wunder allows multiple entry points, but absolutely forbids these entries from growing into multiple independent runtimes.

pages.title: Scheduled Tasks
pages.summary: The Scheduled Tasks core explains why Wunder treats time-driven background tasks as a first-class system capability rather than leaving them to ad-hoc external scripts.

pages.title: Multi-user Management
pages.summary: The Multi-user Management core explains why Wunder must build organization, user, permission, and token account governance into the system skeleton from day one, rather than bolting it on later.

pages.title: Realtime
pages.summary: The realtime core explains why Wunder must continuously project thread changes to the outside world, and strictly distinguishes between event projection and backend truth.

pages.title: Stability
pages.summary: The Stability core explains why Wunder must use structural governance to control fault propagation rather than relying on prompts, luck, and manual monitoring for stability.

pages.title: Observability
pages.summary: The observability core explains how Wunder splits "what happened, why, and how to review" into three distinct semantic layers: the fact layer, replay layer, and profile layer.

pages.title: Integration Overview
pages.summary: Wunder's integration surface includes a unified execution entry, chat domain, workspace, channels, A2A, and MCP; pick the right layer first for a more stable integration.

pages.title: wunder API
pages.summary: `/wunder` is the unified execution entry point, suitable for capability calls; when building a complete chat product, prefer `/wunder/chat/*`.

pages.title: Chat WebSocket
pages.summary: `/wunder/chat/ws` is Wunder's primary real-time chat channel; real-time sessions prefer WS, with SSE as fallback.

pages.title: Chat Sessions
pages.summary: `/wunder/chat/sessions/*` is Wunder's primary chat domain, responsible for session lifecycle, message sending/receiving, attachment preprocessing, event recovery, and runtime management.

pages.title: Workspace API
pages.summary: /wunder/workspace* is not just a file interface - it determines which isolated workspace files are stored in.

pages.title: Temp Directory and Document Conversion
pages.summary: `/wunder/temp_dir/*` handles temporary upload, download, and transit; document conversion and chat attachment preprocessing are handled by separate public conversion, debug conversion, and chat-domain conversion endpoints.

pages.title: User World
pages.summary: `/wunder/user_world/*` is the user-to-user communication domain in Wunder, existing in parallel with the user-to-agent conversation domain.

pages.title: Channel Webhook
pages.summary: Wunder uses /wunder/channel/{provider}/webhook as a unified inbound entry for external channels, then hands off to ChannelHub and the outbox for processing.

pages.title: WeChat iLink Channel
pages.summary: `weixin` is Wunder's new WeChat channel (iLink protocol), different from `wechat` / `wechat_mp`; this article covers selection, QR code integration, runtime checks, and troubleshooting.

pages.title: External Login and Seamless Embedding
pages.summary: Wunder reserves `/wunder/auth/external/*` as the interface for external system embedding and passwordless access.

pages.title: A2A Interface
pages.summary: Wunder exposes A2A JSON-RPC standard access via /a2a and provides a capability discovery entry through AgentCard.

pages.title: MCP Endpoint
pages.summary: Wunder supports both a self-hosted MCP service at `/wunder/mcp` and external MCP service integration.

pages.title: Interface Overview
pages.summary: Wunder currently maintains three interface surfaces — the user-side frontend, the admin frontend, and the desktop client. They share the same backend capabilities but serve different responsibilities.

pages.title: User Frontend
pages.summary: The user-side frontend lives in `frontend/`, unified into a single Messenger workbench that covers profile, chat, swarm, workspace, and system settings.

pages.title: Admin Interface
pages.summary: The admin frontend lives in `web/`, handling models, tools, users, channels, and runtime governance.

pages.title: Desktop Interface
pages.summary: `wunder-desktop` is the current primary delivery form, emphasizing local-first and desktop workbench.

pages.title: Operations Overview
pages.summary: Wunder operations covers deployment, storage, security, channel runtime, and observability. The goal is sustainable, stable operation -- not just "it started successfully."

pages.title: Deployment and Running
pages.summary: Before deploying Wunder, distinguish between desktop, local development, and server paths, then discuss startup commands.

pages.title: Data and Storage
pages.summary: Wunder's persistence requires distinguishing between databases, workspaces, vector storage, and temporary directories.

pages.title: Desktop Local Mode
pages.summary: Desktop local mode is not a scaled-down server, but a local-first, persistent-directory-first runtime form.

pages.title: Authentication and Security
pages.summary: Wunder needs to distinguish between API Keys, user tokens, external auth, and tool execution boundaries.

pages.title: Monitoring and Benchmark
pages.summary: Wunder's observability surface is not a single monitoring page, but composed of session monitoring, tool statistics, performance sampling, throughput stress testing, and benchmark together.

pages.title: Channel Runtime
pages.summary: Channel issues often stem not from the model, but from the access chain: accounts, persistent connections, webhooks, outbox, and download link rewriting.

pages.title: Reference Overview
pages.summary: Reference pages provide stable lookup content and also collect runtime model reference pages broken down by topic, suitable for diving into details after reviewing the core overview.

pages.title: API Index
pages.summary: This is an index page for Wunder's most commonly used interface groups, used for quickly locating endpoints rather than replacing the full API documentation.

pages.title: Configuration
pages.summary: When configuration doesn't take effect, first confirm which layer you're modifying, rather than suspecting the program isn't reading it.

pages.title: Stream Events
pages.summary: When integrating with Wunder's streaming pipeline, the most important thing is not memorizing every event, but knowing which events carry true lifecycle semantics.

pages.title: Workspace Routing
pages.summary: Wunder's workspace routing is not simply directory lookup by user_id; it is determined jointly by `container_id`, agent container configuration, and scoped user_id.

pages.title: Prompt Templates
pages.summary: Wunder splits system prompts into template packs and segment files. Administrators manage system template packs. The user side currently provides two built-in template packs (Chinese and English) by default and supports switching or copying them into custom packs.

pages.title: Admin Panels
pages.summary: The admin-side frontend is not a single debug page, but a set of well-organized governance panels with clear responsibilities.

pages.title: Architecture
pages.summary: wunder uses one unified orchestration kernel to support three runtime forms: server, cli, and desktop.

pages.title: Workspaces and Containers
pages.summary: Wunder's file isolation is not 'a current directory', but a persistent workspace organized by `user_id + container_id` in layers.

pages.title: Sessions and Rounds
pages.summary: wunder splits a conversation into sessions, threads, user rounds, and model rounds.

pages.title: Streaming Execution
pages.summary: Wunder's streaming is not just a character stream, but exposes thread execution, tool calls, and terminal states together.

pages.title: Runtime and Online Status
pages.summary: Wunder doesn't just return message content; it continuously maintains runtime projections of threads and sessions to express states like loaded, waiting, streaming, and idle.

pages.title: Tool System
pages.summary: wunder is not built around "the model answers." It is built around "the model selects and orchestrates tools to finish work."

pages.title: Prompts and Skills
pages.summary: wunder treats system prompts and skills as governed runtime objects rather than temporary text scattered across the codebase.

pages.title: Long-term Memory
pages.summary: wunder's long-term memory design revolves around thread freezing, one-time injection, and structured memory fragments.

pages.title: Token Account and Usage
pages.summary: Wunder uses Token accounts to manage user's disposable balance, and Token usage to observe context load; these are related but not the same thing.

pages.title: Swarm Collaboration
pages.summary: wunder's swarm capability is for multi-agent collaboration, centered on queen orchestration, worker execution, and subagents only when needed.

pages.title: Boundary Handling
pages.summary: wunder's handling strategies and recovery mechanisms for edge cases such as context overflow, network interruption, tool failure, and system exceptions.

pages.title: Help
pages.summary: Start by categorizing symptoms, then follow the troubleshooting path; the goal is to reduce blind log searching and repeated trial-and-error.

pages.title: Troubleshooting
pages.summary: Follow the "entry -> auth -> config -> dependencies -> realtime channel" sequence to quickly locate most Wunder issues.

pages.title: FAQ
pages.summary: Quick answers to high-frequency questions about Wunder, suitable for initial assessment before diving into detailed troubleshooting.

pages.title: Glossary
pages.summary: This page is for quickly standardizing core terminology in Wunder documentation, avoiding mixing user, session, thread, container, and agent into a single concept.

zh-CN/concepts/index.title: 核心概览
zh-CN/concepts/index.summary: 11 个核心不是 11 个功能点，而是 Wunder 为了长期运行、多人并发和多入口接入而主动建立的系统骨架。
read_when: 你第一次系统性理解 Wunder
你准备做接入、运维或工具开发，需要统一视角
你已经会用 Wunder，但想理解它为什么这样设计

headings.text: 为什么这 11 个是核心，而不是普通功能

zh-CN/concepts/core-agent-loop.title: 智能体循环
zh-CN/concepts/core-agent-loop.summary: 智能体循环解释了 Wunder 为什么要把线程当作一等运行单元，以及为什么执行主链路必须稳定、可恢复、可收敛。
read_when: 你想理解线程为什么是 Wunder 的一等运行单元
你在接入、排障或设计工具时需要判断执行链路落点

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：线程要比“当前回答”更重要
重点二：动作必须回到同一条链上
重点三：终态不是一个 UI 感觉，而是系统事实

zh-CN/concepts/core-tools.title: 工具
zh-CN/concepts/core-tools.summary: 工具核心解释了 Wunder 为什么要把能力统一收口成工具，以及为什么工具必须同时面向模型调用、执行治理和前端展示。
read_when: 你在设计内置工具、MCP 或 Skills
你想理解工具返回为什么必须精简明确

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：Wunder 不是只让模型“会说”，而是让模型“会做”
重点二：工具结果要先服务调度，再服务展示
重点三：统一工具体系比“工具越多越好”更重要

zh-CN/concepts/core-swarm.title: 蜂群
zh-CN/concepts/core-swarm.summary: 蜂群核心解释 Wunder 如何把多智能体协作建成正式系统能力，而不是把并行任务临时塞进一个大线程里。
read_when: 你在设计多智能体协作
你需要区分蜂群、子智能体和普通线程链路

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：蜂群管理的是“已有智能体”
重点二：子智能体和蜂群不是同一件事
重点三：协作质量取决于边界清晰，不取决于工蜂数量

zh-CN/concepts/core-context-compression.title: 上下文压缩
zh-CN/concepts/core-context-compression.summary: 上下文压缩核心解释 Wunder 如何在长会话里主动治理上下文规模，并把“压缩”设计成可追溯、可观测、可复盘的正式行为。
read_when: 你在处理长会话或超长工具输出
你想理解压缩为什么不能只是简单摘要

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点二：压缩不是越早越好，也不是越晚越好
重点三：工具 observation 是压缩压力的高发区

zh-CN/concepts/core-memory.title: 记忆
zh-CN/concepts/core-memory.summary: 记忆核心解释 Wunder 为什么坚持“线程初始化注入一次、后续保持冻结”，并把长期资料利用与线程认知稳定性分开治理。
read_when: 你在设计长期记忆、知识库或资料注入策略
你想理解 prompt 冻结和记忆注入的关系

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：记忆的价值不是“记得多”，而是“记得稳”
重点二：初始化注入与运行期检索必须分层
重点三：工作区、知识库、记忆片段不是同一个东西

zh-CN/concepts/core-channels.title: 渠道
zh-CN/concepts/core-channels.summary: 渠道核心解释 Wunder 为什么允许多入口接入，但绝不允许这些入口长成多套独立运行时。
read_when: 你在做多入口接入
你想判断某项能力应该放在内核还是接入层

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：渠道多样化不能破坏线程语义
重点二：接入差异是“面”，不是“骨”
重点三：入口越多，统一口径越重要

zh-CN/concepts/core-scheduled-tasks.title: 定时任务
zh-CN/concepts/core-scheduled-tasks.summary: 定时任务核心解释 Wunder 为什么要把时间驱动的后台任务建成正式能力，而不是把它们丢给外部脚本临时处理。
read_when: 你在设计巡检、提醒、后台维护或延迟执行任务
你想区分在线线程和系统级异步任务

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：把“时间”收进系统，而不是放到系统外面
重点二：定时任务是后台执行链，而不是同步等待链
重点三：调度能力必须天然带执行记录

zh-CN/concepts/core-multi-user-managemen.title: 多用户管理
zh-CN/concepts/core-multi-user-managemen.summary: 多用户管理核心解释 Wunder 为什么必须从一开始就把组织、用户、权限和 Token 账户治理放进系统骨架，而不是后补。
read_when: 你在部署 server 或设计管理员侧
你想理解多用户治理为什么不能后补

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：身份和隔离是两层
重点二：多用户治理一定会下沉到运行时
重点三：管理员能力必须强，但不能模糊边界

zh-CN/concepts/core-realtime.title: 实时性
zh-CN/concepts/core-realtime.summary: 实时性核心解释 Wunder 为什么必须把线程变化持续投影给外部，并严格区分事件投影与后端真相。
read_when: 你在做 WebSocket、流式输出或状态同步
你想理解事件流、快照补偿和断线恢复的边界

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：流式只是实时性的一部分
重点二：实时系统要能“接着看”，不是只能“从头看”
重点三：事件语义要为多端复用设计

zh-CN/concepts/core-stability.title: 稳定性
zh-CN/concepts/core-stability.summary: 稳定性核心解释 Wunder 为什么必须用结构性治理来控制故障扩散，而不是把稳定性寄托在 prompt、好运气和人工盯盘上。
read_when: 你在排障、压测或做高风险链路设计
你想判断某项容错能力应该落在哪一层

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：稳定性首先是“控扩散”
重点二：恢复链要和主链一样正式
重点三：资源治理是稳定性的组成部分

zh-CN/concepts/core-observability.title: 可观测性
zh-CN/concepts/core-observability.summary: 可观测性核心解释 Wunder 如何把“发生了什么、为什么、如何复盘”拆成事实层、回放层和画像层三套明确语义。
read_when: 你在做排障、回放、评测或管理员画像
你想建立统一指标和事件口径

headings.text: 为什么它必须单列为核心
这个核心真正保护什么
重点一：先看事实，再看解释，再看画像
重点二：可观测性要服务两类人
重点三：benchmark、throughput、monitor 不是一回事

Extra input capabilities in local mode

headings.text: If you only read three pages

en/concepts/index.title: Core Concepts
en/concepts/index.summary: The eleven core concepts are not eleven feature bullets. They are the structural boundaries wunder establishes for long-running threads, concurrent users, and multiple entry surfaces.
read_when: You are trying to understand wunder systematically for the first time
You are preparing for integration, operations, or tool development and need one shared perspective
You already know how to use wunder, but want to understand why it is designed this way

headings.text: Why these eleven are core rather than ordinary features
How the eleven core concepts are layered
The eleven core concepts at a glance
Dedicated pages for the eleven core concepts

en/concepts/core-agent-loop.title: Core Agent Loop
en/concepts/core-agent-loop.summary: The Core Agent Loop explains why Wunder treats threads as first-class runtime units, and why the main execution path must be stable, recoverable, and convergent.
read_when: You want to understand why threads are Wunder's first-class runtime units
You need to determine where execution logic should land when integrating, troubleshooting, or designing tools

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core Concept
Priority 1: Thread is more important than "current response"
Priority 2: Actions must return to the same chain
Priority 3: Terminal state is not a UI feeling, it's a system fact

en/concepts/core-tools.title: Tools
en/concepts/core-tools.summary: The Tools core explains why Wunder must unify all capabilities as tools, and why tools must simultaneously serve model invocation, execution governance, and frontend display.
read_when: You are designing built-in tools, MCP, or Skills
You want to understand why tool returns must be concise and clear

headings.text: Why It Must Be Listed as a Core
Focus One: Wunder Doesn't Just Make Models "Talk," It Makes Models "Do"
Focus Two: Tool Results Should Serve Scheduling First, Then Display
Focus Three: A Unified Tool System Is More Important Than "More Tools"

en/concepts/core-swarm.title: Swarm
en/concepts/core-swarm.summary: The Swarm core explains how Wunder builds multi-agent collaboration into a formal system capability, rather than temporarily stuffing parallel tasks into a single large thread.
read_when: You are designing multi-agent collaboration
You need to distinguish between swarms, sub-agents, and ordinary thread flows

headings.text: Why It Must Be Listed as a Core
Focus Two: Sub-agents and Swarms Are Not the Same Thing
Focus Three: Collaboration Quality Depends on Clear Boundaries, Not Worker Count

en/concepts/core-context-compression.title: Context Compression
en/concepts/core-context-compression.summary: The Context Compression core explains how Wunder actively manages context size during long sessions, making compression a traceable, observable, and reviewable formal operation.
read_when: You are handling long sessions or oversized tool outputs
You want to understand why compression cannot be a simple summary

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core
Highlight 3: Tool observations are high-pressure zones

en/concepts/core-memory.title: Memory
en/concepts/core-memory.summary: The Memory core explains why Wunder insists on "inject once during thread initialization, keep frozen afterward", separating long-term data utilization from thread cognitive stability.
read_when: You are designing long-term memory, knowledge base, or data injection strategies
You want to understand the relationship between prompt freezing and memory injection

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core
Highlight 1: The value of memory is "stable recall," not "remembering more"
Highlight 2: Initialization injection and runtime retrieval must be layered
Highlight 3: Workspaces, knowledge bases, and memory fragments are not the same thing

en/concepts/core-channels.title: Core Channels
en/concepts/core-channels.summary: Core Channels explains why Wunder allows multiple entry points, but absolutely forbids these entries from growing into multiple independent runtimes.
read_when: You are integrating multiple entry points
You want to determine whether a capability belongs in the core or the integration layer

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core Concept
Priority 1: Channel diversification cannot break thread semantics
Priority 3: More entries means unified semantics matter more

en/concepts/core-scheduled-tasks.title: Scheduled Tasks
en/concepts/core-scheduled-tasks.summary: The Scheduled Tasks core explains why Wunder treats time-driven background tasks as a first-class system capability rather than leaving them to ad-hoc external scripts.
read_when: You are designing inspection, reminders, background maintenance, or delayed execution tasks
You want to distinguish online threads from system-level async tasks

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Standalone Core
Highlight 1: Bring "time" into the system, not leave it outside
Highlight 2: Scheduled tasks are a background execution chain, not a synchronous wait chain
Highlight 3: Scheduling capability must natively carry execution records

en/concepts/core-multi-user-management.title: Multi-user Management
en/concepts/core-multi-user-management.summary: The Multi-user Management core explains why Wunder must build organization, user, permission, and token account governance into the system skeleton from day one, rather than bolting it on later.
read_when: You are deploying server or designing the admin side
You want to understand why multi-user governance cannot be retrofitted

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core
Highlight 1: Identity and isolation are two separate layers
Highlight 2: Multi-user governance will inevitably sink into the runtime
Highlight 3: Administrator capabilities must be strong, but boundaries must remain clear

en/concepts/core-realtime.title: Realtime
en/concepts/core-realtime.summary: The realtime core explains why Wunder must continuously project thread changes to the outside world, and strictly distinguishes between event projection and backend truth.
read_when: You are working on WebSocket, streaming output, or state synchronization
You want to understand the boundaries of event streams, snapshot compensation, and disconnection recovery

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core Principle
Priority 1: Streaming is only part of realtime
Priority 2: Realtime systems must support "continuing from where you left off", not just "starting from the beginning"
Priority 3: Event semantics must be designed for multi-client reuse

en/concepts/core-stability.title: Stability
en/concepts/core-stability.summary: The Stability core explains why Wunder must use structural governance to control fault propagation rather than relying on prompts, luck, and manual monitoring for stability.
read_when: You are troubleshooting, stress-testing, or designing high-risk execution paths
You want to determine which layer a fault-tolerance capability should belong to

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Standalone Core
Highlight 1: Stability is first about "controlling propagation"
Highlight 2: Recovery paths must be as formal as the main path
Highlight 3: Resource governance is part of stability

en/concepts/core-observability.title: Observability
en/concepts/core-observability.summary: The observability core explains how Wunder splits "what happened, why, and how to review" into three distinct semantic layers: the fact layer, replay layer, and profile layer.
read_when: You are working on troubleshooting, replay, evaluation, or admin profiling
You want to establish unified metrics and event standards

headings.text: Why It Must Be a Core Principle
Priority 1: Facts first, then interpretations, then profiles
Priority 2: Observability must serve two types of people
Priority 3: benchmark, throughput, and monitor are not the same thing

en/concepts/workspaces.summary: Wunder's file isolation is not 'a current directory', but a persistent workspace organized by `user_id + container_id` in layers.
read_when: You want to understand why files are isolated by user and container
You need to determine the relationship between `agent_id`, `container_id`, and workspace

en/concepts/sessions-and-rounds.title: Sessions and Rounds
en/concepts/sessions-and-rounds.summary: wunder splits a conversation into sessions, threads, user rounds, and model rounds.
read_when: You need to understand session state, event streams, and counting conventions
You need to distinguish user rounds, model rounds, and token occupancy

en/concepts/streaming.title: Streaming Execution
en/concepts/streaming.summary: Wunder's streaming is not just a character stream, but exposes thread execution, tool calls, and terminal states together.
read_when: You are integrating SSE or WebSocket
You are designing a state machine for a chat interface

en/concepts/presence-and-runtime.title: Runtime and Online Status
en/concepts/presence-and-runtime.summary: Wunder doesn't just return message content; it continuously maintains runtime projections of threads and sessions to express states like loaded, waiting, streaming, and idle.
read_when: You are building session lists, status badges, or runtime panels
You are troubleshooting whether a thread is stuck or has finished

en/concepts/tools.title: Tool System
en/concepts/tools.summary: wunder is not built around "the model answers." It is built around "the model selects and orchestrates tools to finish work."

You need to understand tool sources, boundaries, and orchestration

en/concepts/prompt-and-skills.summary: wunder treats system prompts and skills as governed runtime objects rather than temporary text scattered across the codebase.

en/concepts/memory.summary: wunder's long-term memory design revolves around thread freezing, one-time injection, and structured memory fragments.

You need to troubleshoot the relationship between thread memory and the system prompt

en/concepts/quota-and-token-usage.title: Token Account and Usage
en/concepts/quota-and-token-usage.summary: Wunder uses Token accounts to manage user's disposable balance, and Token usage to observe context load; these are related but not the same thing.

You want to understand why the system emphasizes round_usage.total_tokens and context occupancy

en/concepts/swarm.summary: wunder's swarm capability is for multi-agent collaboration, centered on queen orchestration, worker execution, and subagents only when needed.
